perturbation.original_text: All Information identified as confidential and disclosed there under shall remain the sole property of the disclosing party, and the receiving party shall have no interest in or rights thereto except as set forth herein.
perturbation.changed_text: All Information disclosed under this agreement shall become jointly owned by both parties from the moment of disclosure, regardless of whether it is identified as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the principle that confidential information remains the property of the disclosing party. By transferring ownership upon disclosure, the receiving party gains rights that undermine the confidentiality agreement and standard intellectual property law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Copyright Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. C-42
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(1) of the Canadian Copyright Act states that the author of a work is the first owner of the copyright. This clause would transfer the copyright immediately which is in contradiction to this law. The modification stating that information becomes jointly owned, thus contradicting the basic principles of copyright ownership where the creator initially retains ownership unless explicitly transferred by other agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: e person by whom the author was employed shall, in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, be the first owner of the copyright, but where the work is an article or other contribution to a newspaper, magazine or similar periodical, there shall, in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, be deemed to be reserved to the author a right to restrain the publication of the work, otherwise than as part of a newspaper, magazine or similar periodical. Marginal note: Assignment of right of action (6) Â For greater certainty, it is deemed always to have been the law that a right of action for infringement of copyright may be assigned in association with the assignment of the copyright or the grant of an interest in the copyright by licence. C-42, s. 13 1997, c. 24, s. 10 2012, c. 20, s. 7 Previous Version Table of Contents 1 ... 2 2.1 ... 6.1 6.2 ... 15 16 ... 20 21 ... 27 27.1 ... 29.4 29.5 ... 30.03 30.04 ... 30.6 30.61 ... 31.1 32 ... 32.5 32.6 ... 38.1 38.2 ... 41.13 41.14 ... 41.25 41.26 ... 44.01 44.02 ... 44.12 44.2 ... 57 58 ... 66.1 66.2 ... 68 68.1 ... 72 72.1 ... 79 80 ... 88 89 ... 92 SCHEDULE I : Existing Rights SCHEDULE II SCHEDULE III Related Provisions Amendments not in force Previous Amendments ï»¿ Date modified: 2025-07-08 ï»¿
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts copyright law by stating that all information disclosed under the agreement becomes jointly owned, regardless of confidentiality. This opposes the principle that the author or disclosing party initially owns the copyright unless there is an agreement to the contrary, as supported by Section 13(1) of the Canadian Copyright Act, mentioned in the Law Explanation, and reinforced by Scraped Law Snippet 1 which states '...in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, be the first owner of the copyright...'. The modification effectively transfers copyright immediately, violating the owner's initial rights.

perturbation.original_text: When receiving the Information each party agrees to maintain all such Information in strict confidence and to use at least the same measures to protect the same as that party uses to protect its own confidential information, including but not limited to: restricting the information’s availability to employees, agents or consultants of the receiving party with a need to know, and making such disclosure only after they have agreed to abide by the terms and conditions of this Agreement, prohibiting duplication of any such information without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, and taking all necessary and reasonable precautions to prevent unauthorised disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: When receiving the Information each party agrees to make reasonable efforts to maintain the confidentiality of such Information. The receiving party has the discretion to decide what constitutes 'reasonable efforts'.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'strict confidence' with 'reasonable efforts' and granting the receiving party discretion creates ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This contradicts the expected standard of care required for confidential information, which typically involves specific and verifiable measures.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law - Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: While not codified in a specific statute, the Ontario Law of Confidence requires a high degree of obligation when dealing with confidential information, often exceeding 'reasonable efforts'. 'Reasonable efforts' can be interpretable but is usually accompanied by strict steps. This modification lowers the bar for confidentiality, potentially conflicting with the legal expectation that the receiving party maintain a high level of protection. (Common law precedent from the UK case Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41 applies in Ontario).

perturbation.justification: The original contract language requires 'strict confidence' and specific measures to protect information. The changed language uses 'reasonable efforts' and grants the receiving party discretion, weakening the protection of confidential information. This contradicts the expectation of a high degree of obligation when dealing with confidential information, as outlined in the Law Explanation which references Ontario Law of Confidence, and the UK case Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41. Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally outlines duties, rights, and obligations of executors and administrators, and the administration of assets, listing things such as 'Rights and liabilities of administrator,' and 'Real and personal estate of deceased are assets for payment of debts.' The modified language doesn't directly violate these statutes, but the change in wording weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, which can affect how such information is handled within these legally defined contexts, creating a legal contradiction. The change introduces ambiguity and reduces the required standard of care, making it more difficult to enforce the confidentiality provisions, especially if the receiving party's 'reasonable efforts' are minimal. This constitutes a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties each agree not to undertake or have undertaken any analysis on the products or the technology supplied by the other parties to determine structure or composition or otherwise to perform tests not authorised by other party without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties each agree they can undertake any analysis on the products or the technology supplied by the other parties as long as they don't use the results commercially against the other party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification permits unauthorized analysis, provided the results are not used commercially against the disclosing party. This introduces ambiguity around the scope of permitted use and undermines the protection of trade secrets inherent in the original clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Trade-marks Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. T-13
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly about analysis, Section 7(e) of the Canadian Trade-marks Act prohibits making false or misleading statements tending to discredit the business, goods, or services of a competitor. Allowing unauthorized analysis, even if not used 'commercially,' creates a higher risk of such statements, which could be a breach of this Act if the analysis leads to disparaging or misleading information about the other party's products or technology. The 'commercial use' exception does not protect against this potential violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Trademarks Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Marginal note: Prohibitions 7 Â No person shall (a) Â make a false or misleading statement tending to discredit the business, goods or services of a competitor; (b) Â direct public attention to his goods, services or business in such a way as to cause or be likely to cause confusion in Canada, at the time he commenced so to direct attention to them, between his goods, services or business and the goods, services or business of another; (c) Â pass off other goods or services as and for those ordered or requested; or (d) Â make use, in association with goods or services, of any description that is false in a material respect and likely to mislead the public as to (i) Â the character, quality, quantity or composition, (ii) Â the geographical origin, or (iii) Â the mode of the manufacture, production or performance of the goods or services. (e) Â [Repealed, 2014, c. 32, s. 10] R.S., 1985, c. T-13, s. 7 2014, c. 32, ss. 10, 53, 56(F) Previous Version Table of Contents 1 ... 7 8 ... 11.12 11.13 ... 11.17 11.18 ... 12 13 ... 27 28 ... 38 38.1 ... 47 47.1 ... 51.03 51.04 ... 51.13 52 ... 61 62 ... 71 72 ... 73 SCHEDULE Related Provisions Previous Amendments ï»¿ Date modified: 2025-07-08 ï»¿
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the spirit, if not the exact letter, of Section 7(a) of the Trademarks Act, as it increases the risk of 'false or misleading statement tending to discredit the business, goods or services of a competitor.' The original text prevented unauthorized analysis, thus mitigating the risk of disparaging statements based on potentially flawed or biased analysis. By permitting analysis as long as it is not used commercially, the changed text increases the risk of generating and disseminating misleading information, even if not directly for commercial gain, which could still discredit a competitor as described in snippet 1. The 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition states that the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable, is also applicable here as the modified text weakens the original protection against potential harm, thus contradicting legal norms.
